<timestamp>Saturday, May 16, 2026, 12:05 PM (UTC+5:30)</timestamp>
<user_query>
now I want you to understand the whole problem solving coach repo/project and explain me about it in detail what value it adds to a person who solves this and what valule does this adds to person who monitors our work like VP, CEO, CTO what they are looking for from us by solving this repo. basically this repo is given to Intern/freshers when they enter into the company.
</user_query>
I should start by diving into the problem-solving-coach repository to understand what it does and what value it provides.

input.command: ls coaching; echo "---"; ls results; echo "---"; ls .cursor
ls coaching\intro; echo "---"; ls coaching\solving; echo "---"; ls coaching\understanding-problems; echo "---"; ls results\understanding-problems; echo "---"; ls .cursor\rules
ls coaching\solving | findstr decomposition; echo "---"; ls coaching\solving

content.text: I'm noticing that the index references a file that doesn't actually exist, so I need to look at the decomposition module files to understand what methods are being covered.

Now I'm getting a better picture of the repo structure. Let me explore the Trilogy connection and review a few more modules like the disagree-commit-trust and problem-tree components to make sure I have the full context.

# The Problem Solving Coach repo — a complete walkthrough

I've read through the rule file, the master index, the README, and a representative slice of every track (intro, problem-understanding examples, restating, inputs/outputs/constraints, deep requirements, clarifying questions, disagree-commit-trust, problem-tree intro + modules, decomposition modules, and your existing `results/`). Here's the full picture.

---

## 1. What this repo actually is (mechanically)

It is **not** a codebase. It is a **Cursor-powered, AI-driven coaching curriculum** that turns Cursor's chat into a personal trainer for the non-technical, judgment-level skills that separate good engineers from coders.

The mechanism is clever and minimal:

- `.cursor/rules/coach.mdc` is an `alwaysApply: true` Cursor rule. It instructs the AI: *"You are a coach. Read the modules in `coaching/index.md`, copy that index into `results/`, teach the lessons one by one following each module's `Coach instructions`, tick off completed lessons, write a summary per lesson, and finally commit to a branch named after the participant and push to origin."*
- `coaching/index.md` is the **master curriculum** (do-not-edit).
- `coaching/**.md` are the **lesson files**, each with explicit `Coach instructions` (objective, format, focus, time, rubrics) so the AI behaves consistently no matter who runs it.
- `results/index.md` is your **personal progress file** (already started — your `disagree-commit-trust-intro.md` summary is on branch `bharath-ti`).
- `results/understanding-problems/*.md` are **per-lesson summaries** the coach writes as you go.

You interact entirely through Cursor chat in agent mode — say "Hi", the coach takes over, and you progress through ~4–6 hours of guided exercises.

---

## 2. The curriculum, mapped to one big idea

The whole course is built on **George Polya's 4 steps** from *How to Solve It* (`coaching/intro/the-steps.md`):

1. Understand the Problem  
2. Devise a Plan  
3. Carry Out the Plan  
4. Look Back / Reflect

Everything in the repo is a deep dive into **Step 1 and Step 2**, because the company has decided those are exactly where freshers fail.

### Track A — Problem Understanding (the bulk of the course)

| Sub-track | What it drills into your head |
|---|---|
| **Two cautionary tale examples** (`db-example.md`, `performance-optimization-example.md`) | If you skip understanding and "just code", you waste days, leak PII, optimize the wrong bottleneck, and burn stakeholder trust. These are the emotional hook. |
| **Restating Problems** (5 modules) | Force yourself to rephrase what the stakeholder said, expose assumptions, separate facts from gaps. |
| **Inputs / Outputs / Constraints** (3 modules) | Define exactly what flows in, what must come out, and what limits the solution — before designing anything. |
| **Deep Requirements** (3 modules) | The "iceberg" mindset — why do they *really* want this? What's the business/user motivation under the surface request? |
| **Clarifying Questions** (3 modules) | A framework (*What is the unknown? What are the data? What is the condition?*) for asking the few questions that matter, not all of them. |
| **Have Backbone; Disagree and Commit (with Earn Trust)** (3 modules) | **Conversation Mode** — the coach role-plays a frustrated PM, an impatient CEO ("how hard can it be?"), and contradictory Ops vs Sales stakeholders. You get scored on tone, risk-naming, business translation, options, and commitment hygiene. |

### Track B — Problem Solution / Breaking Down Complexity

| Sub-track | What it drills |
|---|---|
| **Problem Tree Method** (intro + 3 modules + `problem-tree-patterns-quickref.md`) | A disciplined decomposition technique: every node is either a *bounded, decision-oriented question* or a concrete answer; every level must completely answer its parent. Patterns include Decision Tree, Who/Why/What/How, 5W1H, Current/Ideal/Gap/How, Stakeholder/Process/Obstacle/Action, Assumption Pattern. |
| **Alternative Decomposition Methods** (6 modules) | Architecture Sketch competition, Overwhelm-to-Relief chunking, Recipe Reverse-Engineering, Modular Lego, Decomposition Archaeology, Assembly Line. Each gives you a *different mental metaphor* for breaking down complexity, so you don't get stuck with only one tool. |

---

## 3. What the participant (intern/fresher) actually gains

These aren't abstract benefits — they map directly to what the lesson files practice:

1. **A repeatable mental model.** Polya's 4 steps + restating + IOC + clarifying questions + problem trees becomes a checklist that triggers automatically whenever a ticket lands. You stop "diving in".
2. **The "understand before you build" reflex.** The two cautionary scenarios (`db-example`, `performance-optimization-example`) are designed to *traumatize* you mildly so you never forget. After this, "ASAP" no longer makes you skip discovery — you learn that *"ASAP" means the right thing ASAP*.
3. **Stakeholder-conversation skills under pressure.** The disagree-and-commit conversation modules are unusually advanced for a fresher curriculum — you literally rehearse pushing back on a PM who's already promised the board, and a CEO who's mid-sales-cycle. You leave with phrases you can reuse in real meetings the next week.
4. **Decomposition as a muscle.** After modules 1–6 of decomposition, an "enterprise platform with 30+ features" wall-of-text becomes 4 domains → MVP in five minutes. This is the single biggest gap between a junior and a senior IC.
5. **Question-writing as a tradable skill.** You learn the difference between *"Why is our API slow?"* (useless) and *"Which 3 checkout queries take >2s and affect the most users?"* (decision-driving). That distinction is what your tech leads quietly judge you on.
6. **Customer/business translation.** Every "Deep Requirements" and "Disagree-Commit" module pushes you to talk in *renewal, revenue, reputation, churn, support load* — not in *threads, caches, indexes*. That habit is what gets juniors promoted.
7. **Deliberate practice using Cursor agent mode.** You learn Cursor as a *thinking partner*, not as autocomplete. That is itself a Trilogy/Crossover-grade work skill — they run AI-leveraged dev shops and want you fluent on day one.
8. **A written artifact of your judgment.** Every module produces a summary you wrote (or co-wrote with the coach) and pushes it to a branch named after you. You finish the week with a portable record of *how you think*.

---

## 4. What VP / CEO / CTO are really looking for when they grade this

Because the coach commits everything to **a branch named after you** and pushes to origin, leadership doesn't have to ask "did you do the training?" — they can read your `results/` folder, your summaries, your one-line takeaways, and judge the *quality* of your reflection. Here is what they're scanning for:

1. **Judgment signal, not code signal.** A fresher who codes blindly is *net negative* on a team for 6 months. They want evidence you'll stop, ask, and scope. The two trap-scenarios (DB report, performance opt) are literal IQ tests on this.
2. **Communication maturity under stakeholder pressure.** The disagree-and-commit rubrics score you on *Earn Trust, Backbone, Specificity, Options, Decision Hygiene, Commit Readiness*. These are leadership principles (heavily modeled on Amazon's). Leadership wants to know: under heat, will you stonewall, capitulate, or partner? Your debrief summaries reveal this.
3. **Customer/business orientation.** The thing that separates an engineer who plateaus at L4 from one who reaches L6 is *translation*. Every module pushes you to talk in business terms. Your summaries either show that muscle or don't.
4. **Decomposition under overwhelm.** The "Overwhelm to Relief" module literally measures whether you panic at a wall of requirements or chunk it. That is what senior ICs do all day.
5. **Self-direction.** It is 4–6 hours of self-paced training with only an AI as your guide. Completing it cleanly, on a clearly named branch, with honest tick-marks (no skipped modules) is itself a signal of ownership.
6. **Cultural fit with the operating system of the company.** Trilogy-style companies run on principles very close to Amazon's Leadership Principles: *Customer Obsession* (Deep Requirements), *Dive Deep* (Clarifying Questions), *Have Backbone, Disagree and Commit*, *Earn Trust*, *Bias for Action with rigor* (bounded questions, MVP). This repo is essentially **a cultural induction disguised as a problem-solving course**. Doing it well telegraphs "this person will fit our cadence."
7. **AI leverage proficiency.** The fact that the curriculum is delivered *through Cursor agent mode* is itself the test. They want to see you treat the AI as a Socratic coach, not a code-vending-machine. The way you converse with the coach in your transcripts is itself an evaluation.
8. **Coachability and reflection.** Each module ends with a reflection question (*"What's one phrase you'll reuse next week under pressure?"*, *"What's your personal rule for when you will vs won't give a date?"*). Your written answers in `results/` are read as a tiny window into your thinking. A thoughtful one-liner there is worth more than 100 lines of code.

---

## 5. How to extract maximum value (practical advice for you specifically)

Looking at your current state — only `disagree-commit-trust-intro` is ticked on branch `bharath-ti` — here's how to make the rest count:

- **Go in order.** The curriculum is intentionally cumulative (restate → IOC → deep req → clarifying → disagree-commit; question-quality → tree-construction → larger trees; decomposition 1 → 6).
- **Don't speed-run the trap scenarios.** Choose the *wrong* answer once on purpose in `db-example.md` and `performance-optimization-example.md` — read the consequence. That emotional hit is the entire point.
- **Treat the conversation-mode modules like rehearsal, not multiple choice.** Type the actual sentences you'd say. Let "Alex" and "Jordan" push back. The rubric is what your future tech lead will silently use on you in a real meeting.
- **Write your own summaries**, not just whatever the coach drafts. The `results/` file is what leadership reads — make it yours.
- **Keep your branch (`bharath-ti`) clean and push regularly** so progress is visible. The coach rule already does this for you; just don't override it.
- **When you finish, re-read `results/index.md` end-to-end.** It is now a personal cheat sheet you can refer to in real tickets.

---

## 6. One-sentence summary

This repo is **the company's bet that the fastest way to turn a fresher into a useful engineer is not to teach more tech, but to drill the judgment, communication, and decomposition habits that determine whether the tech they already know creates value or burns it** — and the `results/` branch you push back is leadership's window into whether that bet is paying off on you.

 I'm getting a clear picture of what this repo is—it's an onboarding curriculum for Trilogy interns that leverages Cursor's Coach feature to create an AI-powered interactive teaching experience focused on professional and soft skills development.

The mechanism is straightforward: a Coach rule configures Cursor's AI as an instructor, the curriculum lives in markdown modules that the coach reads sequentially, and progress gets tracked and pushed to a branch so leadership can monitor participant growth. The curriculum itself is built around Polya's problem-solving framework, starting with understanding before jumping to solutions—covering problem decomposition, communication skills, and the disagree-and-commit leadership principle that Trilogy values. The whole approach is interactive rather than lecture-based, using exercises, multiple choice, and conversation modes to build both analytical thinking and the communication backbone needed to handle stakeholders effectively.

For interns and freshers, this teaches a universal mental model that applies beyond just coding—the habit of asking clarifying questions and understanding requirements deeply before building saves enormous wasted effort. It also builds the structured decomposition skills to turn overwhelming asks into clear plans, and practices using AI tools deliberately, which is a core workplace skill. The audit trail in the results folder becomes a reusable reference of their thinking process.

What leadership is really evaluating is whether a fresher can demonstrate judgment and restraint—stopping to think and ask before coding, rather than shipping blindly. They're looking for communication maturity under pressure, especially in those disagree-and-commit scenarios where a PM or CEO is pushing back. They want to see customer and business orientation, the ability to translate technical constraints into revenue and reputation language. And they're assessing decomposition ability—can someone take a vague enterprise project and produce a focused MVP plan in a reasonable timeframe.

The Cursor proficiency matters because these companies are AI-leverage shops, and this repo is deliberate practice with agent mode workflows. Leadership will read the results folder directly—your one-line summaries, branch name, whether you marked items honestly, and your debrief responses—to judge if you reflect, stay coachable, and think clearly. The curriculum itself mirrors Amazon Leadership Principles, so completing it well signals cultural fit. And the fact that you're working through 4–6 hours self-paced with only an AI coach, without hand-holding, is itself a signal of self-direction and ownership.
